items.title: Youth Leadership Academy
items.desc: Equipping young Rwandans with leadership, critical thinking, and entrepreneurship skills through a rigorous 12-month training program.
items.body:
The Youth Leadership Academy is RAPP's flagship education programme, launched in 2022 in partnership with the Musanze District local government. It targets young Rwandans aged 18–30 who have completed secondary school but lack pathways to meaningful employment or civic engagement.

What the Programme Delivers

The Academy runs as a full-time, 12-month residential programme combining classroom learning with community practicum. Students spend four days a week in structured coursework and one day a week applying their learning in a real community project — designing a solution to a local challenge, presenting to community stakeholders, and measuring their own impact.

The curriculum is built around five pillars: critical thinking, ethical leadership, entrepreneurship and financial literacy, civic engagement, and peace and conflict resolution. Every pillar connects RAPP's core mission to the practical skills young Rwandans need to lead.

The Entrepreneurship Track

One of the Academy's most distinctive features is its entrepreneurship track. Students who complete the leadership core can apply for a 6-month business incubation extension, during which they receive seed funding (up to 500,000 RWF), mentorship from established Rwandan entrepreneurs, and access to RAPP's network of business partners and micro-finance institutions.

To date, 34 student-founded businesses have launched through this track, creating an estimated 90 additional jobs in the Musanze economy.

Results After Three Years

Since launching in 2022, the Academy has graduated three cohorts totalling 514 students. Follow-up surveys conducted 12 months after graduation show that 87% of graduates are in formal employment, running their own business, or engaged in recognised community leadership — compared to a baseline of 31% at programme entry. Graduates have gone on to serve in district government, lead community health committees, found NGOs, and mentor the cohorts that followed them.

Expanding the Model

Following the success of the Musanze campus, RAPP is currently constructing a second Academy site in Huye, scheduled to open in Q3 2025. A third site in Kayonza is in feasibility study. The vision: a nationwide network of Youth Leadership Academies, each rooted in its local community while sharing a common curriculum and quality standard, producing a generation of Rwandan leaders equipped to drive the country's next decade of progress.

items.title: Dialogue for Reconciliation
items.desc: Facilitating structured dialogue sessions between communities to heal wounds of the past and build foundations for lasting peace.
items.body:
Dialogue for Reconciliation is the programme that sits at the very heart of RAPP's identity. Launched in 2018, it is built on a simple but demanding premise: that lasting peace is not built by governments alone, or through formal treaties, but through the painstaking, deeply human work of communities choosing to face each other, tell the truth, and decide to move forward together.

The Dialogue Methodology

RAPP's dialogue sessions follow a structured methodology developed over years of practice and refined in collaboration with international peacebuilding experts, Rwandan psychologists, and community elders. Each dialogue process runs across six sessions held over three months, allowing trust to build gradually between participants.

Sessions are facilitated by RAPP-trained community facilitators — not external experts — who are drawn from the communities themselves. This is deliberate: sustainable reconciliation cannot be imported. It must be owned by the people who live it.

What Happens in the Room

The sessions move through distinct phases: opening (establishing safety and shared agreements), testimony (structured sharing of personal experiences of the conflict), acknowledgement (recognising harm without yet requiring forgiveness), envisioning (articulating a shared future both parties want), and commitment (identifying concrete joint actions to build that future).

Not every process reaches its final phase. Some communities need multiple cycles. Some dialogues surface new layers of pain that require additional support before they can move forward. RAPP's approach honours that complexity rather than rushing toward a pre-scripted reconciliation moment.

Seven Years of Impact

Since 2018, the programme has facilitated more than 200 structured dialogue processes involving over 12,000 participants across 85 communities in all five provinces. Post-dialogue surveys, conducted six months after completion, consistently show significant improvements in inter-group trust, willingness to cooperate on community projects, and reported sense of personal peace and healing among participants.

Twelve communities that completed the dialogue process have gone on to establish joint community development committees — governance structures that continue to function years after RAPP's direct involvement ended.

Training the Next Generation of Facilitators

One of the programme's proudest achievements is its facilitator training pipeline. To date, 148 community members have been trained as certified RAPP dialogue facilitators, and 37 of them have gone on to facilitate processes independently, without RAPP's direct involvement. The methodology is becoming self-replicating — exactly as intended.

items.title: Hillside Farmers Cooperative
items.desc: Organizing smallholder farmers into cooperatives with access to modern tools, market linkages, and climate-smart farming techniques.
items.body:
The Hillside Farmers Cooperative programme ran from 2019 to 2023 in Rwanda's Southern Province, where steep, terraced hillsides are both the landscape's defining feature and one of its greatest agricultural challenges. Working with smallholder farmers who were managing their land in isolation, RAPP helped organise 14 formal cooperatives and connected them to markets, modern inputs, and climate-smart farming knowledge that transformed their yields — and their livelihoods.

The Challenge of Smallholder Farming

Rwanda's Southern Province is home to thousands of smallholder farmers, most tending plots of less than half a hectare on heavily terraced hillsides. Individually, these farmers faced a common set of obstacles: limited access to quality seeds and fertilisers, no leverage to negotiate fair prices with buyers, no insurance against crop failure, and farming practices that, while deeply familiar, were increasingly ill-suited to the changing rainfall patterns brought by climate change.

What RAPP Did

RAPP's approach was to work with existing social networks — neighbours who already knew and trusted each other — to form cooperatives of 80–120 farmers each. Once legally registered, each cooperative gained access to RAPP's programme benefits: bulk purchasing of inputs at reduced cost, collective negotiation with buyers and processors, access to a cooperative savings and loan scheme, and a 12-week climate-smart agriculture training facilitated by Rwanda Agriculture and Animal Resources Development Board agronomists.

Impact at Scale

By the programme's end in 2023, 14 cooperatives had been established with a combined membership of 1,247 farming families. Average household agricultural income across cooperative members increased by 45% over the programme period. Three cooperatives established direct supply agreements with Kigali-based food processors, providing guaranteed market access that removed the uncertainty that had plagued individual farmers for generations.

The programme also demonstrated measurable environmental impact: participating farms showed a 28% reduction in topsoil erosion, attributed to the adoption of improved terracing techniques and the introduction of cover crops.

Legacy: What Remains

Though RAPP's direct involvement ended in 2023, 12 of the 14 cooperatives remain active and self-sustaining. Three have expanded their membership. The cooperative model has been adopted by Rwanda's Ministry of Agriculture as a case study in its smallholder development strategy. Most importantly, 1,200 farming families who were isolated and vulnerable are now organised, connected, and building a more secure future together.

items.title: Community Health Worker Network
items.desc: Training and deploying community health workers to provide first-line healthcare services in remote areas.
items.body:
Launched in 2021, the Community Health Worker (CHW) Network is RAPP's largest active health programme and one of the most impactful in its history. Working in Rwanda's Eastern Province — the country's largest, with significant geographic barriers to formal healthcare — the programme has trained and deployed 340 community health workers who together reached 80,000 people in 2024 alone.

Why Community Health Workers

Community health workers are one of the most evidence-based, cost-effective interventions in global health. Trained community members who are trusted by their neighbours, they reach people who would otherwise never see a healthcare professional — detecting illness earlier, providing basic treatment, making timely referrals, and delivering health education that changes behaviour at the household level.

In Rwanda's Eastern Province, where a single community health centre may serve tens of thousands of people spread across dozens of villages connected by rough roads, CHWs are not a supplement to the health system. For many people, they are the health system.

Training and Equipment

RAPP's CHW training programme is a rigorous 12-week residential course covering preventive care, maternal and child health, malnutrition screening and treatment, basic first aid and wound care, disease surveillance and reporting, and the use of a digital health tracking application developed specifically for the programme. Each CHW graduates with a standardised field kit, a smartphone pre-loaded with the health app, and a monthly supply of essential medicines and oral rehydration salts.

Digital Innovation

The programme's digital health tracking system — built in partnership with a Kigali-based health technology startup — has been one of its most important innovations. The app allows CHWs to log patient visits and outcomes, flag urgent cases for health centre follow-up, access treatment guidance offline, and contribute to a real-time disease surveillance dashboard used by district health authorities. In 2024, the system helped identify and respond to two localised cholera outbreaks three weeks earlier than would have been possible through traditional reporting channels.

Looking Forward

In 2025, RAPP will expand the CHW Network to two additional districts and introduce a peer supervision model in which senior CHWs mentor and oversee newer recruits. A community health insurance linkage pilot will begin in Q2 2025, connecting CHW clients to Rwanda's Mutuelle de Santé programme and reducing the financial barriers that keep the most vulnerable away from formal care. The goal: 150,000 people reached by the end of 2026.

items.title: Reforestation Rwanda
items.desc: Restoring degraded hillsides through tree planting campaigns while creating sustainable livelihoods for local communities.
items.body:
Reforestation Rwanda is one of RAPP's most visible and beloved programmes — a campaign that has, since 2020, planted more than half a million trees across Rwanda's Western Province, with a target of one million by 2027. But as with all of RAPP's work, the trees are only part of the story.

The Problem: Degraded Hillsides, Vulnerable Communities

Rwanda's Western Province includes some of the most ecologically significant terrain in East Africa, but decades of subsistence farming and charcoal production have left many hillsides stripped of their natural cover. The consequences are cascading: without tree roots to hold the soil, heavy rains trigger erosion and landslides; agricultural plots lose their fertility; water sources become unreliable; and communities that depend on the land face deepening insecurity.

Reforestation is not simply an environmental intervention — it is a community resilience intervention, a food security intervention, and a climate adaptation intervention all at once.

The Green Guardian Model

Reforestation Rwanda's most important innovation is its Green Guardian model. Rather than bringing in external teams to plant trees, RAPP trains and employs community members — with a deliberate focus on women and youth — as paid Green Guardians: trained tree planters, nursery managers, and forest monitors who receive a monthly stipend for their work and develop marketable environmental management skills.

To date, 1,400 Green Guardians have been trained and employed across 38 communities in the Western Province. The model ensures that reforestation creates immediate economic benefit for the communities doing the work, building their stake in the trees' long-term survival.

The Species Strategy

Working with Rwanda's Forestry Authority and international botanists, RAPP developed a planting strategy that balances ecological restoration with economic utility. Approximately 60% of planting uses native species — restoring biodiversity and providing long-term carbon sequestration and watershed protection. The remaining 40% uses fast-growing economically valuable species that provide near-term timber and sustainable charcoal income, reducing pressure on remaining natural forests.

School and Faith Community Partnerships

A key driver of the programme's scale has been partnerships with 47 schools and 12 faith communities across the Western Province. Each school maintains a school nursery that produces seedlings for the wider campaign, integrating environmental education into the school curriculum while contributing materially to the reforestation effort. Faith community leaders have been among the programme's most powerful advocates, framing tree-planting as a moral and spiritual obligation — a reframing that has dramatically expanded participation among older community members.

The Road to One Million

With 500,000 trees in the ground and the campaign expanding to the Northern Province in 2025, the target of one million trees by 2027 is firmly in sight. A new digital tree-tracking platform, launching in mid-2025, will allow donors and community members to follow the growth of individual trees through satellite imagery and Green Guardian field reports — creating a direct connection between global support and local impact.

items.title: Women's Economic Empowerment
items.desc: Providing micro-loans, business training, and mentorship to women entrepreneurs to grow their businesses and improve their families' well-being.
items.body:
RAPP's Women's Economic Empowerment programme was launched in 2021 with a clear-eyed recognition that Rwanda's remarkable development story will remain incomplete unless women — who make up 52% of the population and shoulder a disproportionate share of both productive and reproductive labour — are at its centre. Since launch, the programme has supported 350 women entrepreneurs across Kigali and the Northern Province, with results that consistently exceed expectations.

The Core Offer: Capital, Knowledge, and Community

The programme's design rests on three interconnected pillars, each addressing a different barrier that keeps women-led micro-businesses from growing:

  • Micro-loans: Trust-based loans of 50,000–200,000 RWF, repaid over 12 months, with a 95% repayment rate to date. Collateral is replaced by peer group accountability — members of the same cooperative cohort are each other's guarantors.
  • Business training: An 8-month programme covering pricing and costing, record-keeping and financial management, digital payment tools and mobile banking, marketing and customer acquisition, and business planning for growth.
  • Peer learning and mentorship: Monthly cooperative circles where members share challenges and solutions, combined with one-on-one mentorship from established Rwandan businesswomen who volunteer their time through RAPP's mentor network.

What the Numbers Show

Across four cooperative cohorts totalling 350 women, average monthly household income has increased by 180% from programme entry to 12-month follow-up. Three cohort members have grown their businesses to a point where they employ at least one additional worker. One member is exporting handmade goods internationally. The 95% loan repayment rate — achieved without collateral, in a population that formal banks routinely decline to serve — demonstrates both the trustworthiness of participants and the power of peer accountability as a lending mechanism.

Beyond Business: Social Transformation

The programme's most profound impacts are sometimes the hardest to measure. Cohort members consistently report increases in confidence, in their voice within their households, and in their ability to make decisions about their own lives and their children's futures. Several cohorts have, spontaneously, established collective childcare arrangements, shared transport networks, and joint advocacy efforts on market conditions — social infrastructure that emerged from relationships the programme created but that RAPP did not design.

Scaling the Model

A fifth cooperative cohort is planned for Rubavu in mid-2025, and RAPP is in discussions with Rwanda's Ministry of Gender and Family Promotion about incorporating elements of the programme model — particularly the peer learning structure and trust-based lending mechanism — into national women's empowerment policy. The vision is a Rwanda where every woman entrepreneur, regardless of her location or starting point, has access to the capital, knowledge, and community she needs to build something lasting.

items.title: Scenarios from Africa
items.desc: Educating youth about HIV/AIDS prevention through creative media — songs, poems, stories, cartoons, plays, and short films produced with internationally acclaimed directors.
items.body:
Scenarios from Africa began as a way to educate youth about HIV/AIDS prevention through a variety of forms of media. Scenarios holds contests every year where students can submit songs, poems, stories, dialogues, cartoons, plays, and other creative works.

Selected presentations work with internationally acclaimed directors to produce a short movie to present their project, giving young Rwandans a powerful platform to share life-saving messages with their peers and communities.

items.title: Voices of Health Project
items.desc: Using interactive theater to encourage responsible, preventive behaviour around HIV/AIDS, Family Planning, and Sexual Reproductive Health while reducing stigma of people living with HIV.
items.body:
Voices of Health started in 2009 and was funded by naction medeor, a German NGO, until 2013. The project uses interactive theater to encourage a responsible and preventive behavior concerning HIV/AIDS, Family Planning and Sexual Reproductive Health, and at the same time to reduce discrimination and stigmatization of people living with HIV/AIDS (PLWHA).

Community Theater Troupes informed and sensitized approximately 43,200 individuals and community members within four years. Voices of Health also improved the physical, psychosocial, and economic living conditions of 12 cooperatives of PLWHA by assisting with setting up and developing income generating activities — leading to greater acceptance of and demand for their products, increased production, and an improved state of health.

items.title: Global Fund for HIV/AIDS, TB, and Malaria R6
items.desc: Providing vocational training and cooperative development for vulnerable people organized in cooperatives and Anti-AIDS clubs across Rwanda.
items.body: This Global Fund Round 6 project provides vocational training and cooperative development for vulnerable people organized in cooperatives and Anti-AIDS clubs across Rwanda, helping marginalized communities build sustainable livelihoods and resilience.

items.title: Behavior Change and Social Marketing (BCSM)
items.desc: Strengthening the capacity of Rwandan partner organizations, communities, and private sector networks to promote healthy behaviors and increase access to life-saving health products through social marketing.
items.body:
The vision of BCSM is to strengthen the capacity of Rwandan Partner Organizations, communities, and private sector distribution networks to promote healthy behaviors and to increase access to lifesaving health products through social marketing. BCSM is funded with the support of USAID.

120 peer educators trained by RAPP and out-of-school peer educators trained by youth centers conduct outreach activities consisting of regular small discussion groups on topics including role plays. Working with a small professional group of trained theater actors, RAPP creates plays for special events aimed to increase uptake of mobile Voluntary Counseling and Testing (VCT) and promote condom use among target groups of most at-risk youth in 'hot spots', collaborating with the youth center Dushinze.

items.title: In School Project
items.desc: Providing technical training and theatre competitions for school youth clubs focused on HIV/AIDS and Sexual and Reproductive Health education, while empowering Orphans and Vulnerable Children to continue school.
items.body:
The In School Project began in 2004. The project provides technical training and support and theatre competitions for school youth clubs that are focused on educating themselves and their peers about HIV/AIDS and Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights.

The project also empowers Orphans and Vulnerable Children (OVC) affected and/or infected by HIV/AIDS to continue their education, ensuring that no child is left behind due to the impact of the disease on their family.

items.title: Rwanda Knits
items.desc: Providing hand-operated knitting machines, technical training, and business skills to low-income Rwandan women — enabling them to build sustainable income through knitting cooperatives.
items.body:
Rwanda Knits provides hand operated, American-made knitting machines, technical, and business training to low-income Rwandan women. The programme enables them to increase their incomes through economically sustainable knitting cooperatives, through which they produce garments for their domestic market and export markets.

Through a USAID grant, Rwanda Knits has enabled over 1,200 women to earn a living through making and selling their knitted goods. The organization has provided over 600 knitting machines and training to women throughout the country, now forming 29 knitting cooperatives. Out of this, three cooperatives formed the country's first Secondary (umbrella type) Artisan Cooperative — a milestone recognised as one of USAID Rwanda's most successful income producing projects.
